question: What does CSI stand for?
explanation: The Container Storage Interface is a standardized API for storage plugins so vendors can add drivers without forking Kubernetes.

question: Which party typically provides a CSI driver for a given storage system?

options.label: a
options.text: The storage vendor or community (out-of-tree plugin)

explanation: CSI was introduced precisely to move storage drivers out of Kubernetes core. Vendors maintain and ship their own out-of-tree CSI drivers.

question: Which feature of CSI enables taking a point-in-time copy of a volume through Kubernetes APIs?
explanation: The VolumeSnapshot feature (VolumeSnapshotClass, VolumeSnapshot, VolumeSnapshotContent) is standard across CSI drivers that support it.

question: Which feature lets you grow a PVC's storage without recreating the workload (when the CSI driver supports it)?

options.label: a
options.text: Volume expansion via allowVolumeExpansion on the StorageClass

explanation: Setting allowVolumeExpansion: true on the StorageClass, combined with a CSI driver that supports expansion, enables online PVC growth.

question: What is the role of the CSI external-provisioner sidecar?

options.label: a
options.text: It watches PVCs and calls the CSI driver's CreateVolume/DeleteVolume RPCs

options.label: b
options.text: It terminates TLS on the PVC

explanation: CSI sidecars (external-provisioner, attacher, resizer, snapshotter) live beside the CSI driver and bridge Kubernetes APIs to CSI RPCs.

question: Which statement about dynamic provisioning is TRUE?

options.label: a
options.text: A PVC referencing a StorageClass with a provisioner triggers the CSI driver to create a matching PV on demand

options.label: b
options.text: An admin must pre-create every PV

options.label: d
options.text: Dynamic provisioning is only for emptyDir

explanation: Dynamic provisioning is the default modern flow. Static PVs are still possible for pre-existing storage you want to expose.

question: Which CSI feature lets PVCs be scheduled only where topology constraints (zone, Node labels) allow the volume to attach?
explanation: Topology-aware provisioning ensures a block volume is created in the same zone as the Pod that will consume it.

question: Which field on a StorageClass controls whether volumes created from it are deleted or retained when their PVC is released?
explanation: reclaimPolicy on the StorageClass (Delete or Retain) determines whether Kubernetes instructs the CSI driver to delete the backing volume or leave it in place.

question: Which Pod-level volume type fills the gap between container image and real persistent storage, remaining for the life of the Pod?
explanation: emptyDir is created empty at Pod start and destroyed when the Pod ends — ephemeral but Pod-scoped, useful for scratch space.

question: Which of the following is NOT a typical responsibility of a CSI driver?
explanation: CSI is about volume lifecycle. Building images is a CI/registry concern, not CSI.

question: Which CNCF graduated project pairs with CSI to manage cluster backups and restores?
explanation: Velero backs up Kubernetes objects and volume data (via CSI snapshots or restic/Kopia), enabling disaster recovery and migrations.

question: Which access mode is usually supported by block-storage CSI drivers (EBS, GCE PD, Azure Disk) and not by file-storage drivers?
explanation: Block volumes almost always support RWO; multi-Node RWX typically requires a shared filesystem (EFS, NFS, CephFS).

question: What is the purpose of the `ephemeral inline CSI volume` feature?

options.label: a
options.text: Allow CSI drivers to provide ephemeral Pod-scoped volumes defined directly in the Pod spec without a PVC

explanation: Inline CSI volumes enable drivers to serve ephemeral, Pod-bound volumes (e.g., for secret managers, config overlays) without the PV/PVC cycle.

question: Which of the following is a correct path when a workload wants to bring its own cloud-vendor disk into Kubernetes?

options.label: a
options.text: Create a PV that references the existing disk, and create a matching PVC

options.label: b
options.text: Use an init container to attach the disk

options.label: d
options.text: Add the disk to the Pod hostPath

explanation: Static provisioning binds a PVC to a pre-created PV that references a specific disk volumeHandle. It is the standard way to expose existing storage.

question: Which part of the CSI architecture runs on every Node to attach/mount volumes?
explanation: CSI drivers run two logical components: a Controller plugin (typically a Deployment) and a Node plugin (DaemonSet) that handles per-Node attach/mount operations.

question: Why is WaitForFirstConsumer volumeBindingMode recommended for topologically-constrained storage?

options.label: a
options.text: It delays provisioning until the Pod is scheduled, so the volume is created in the correct zone/topology

options.label: d
options.text: It eliminates the need for a PV

explanation: Immediate binding can provision a volume in a zone where no feasible Node exists. WaitForFirstConsumer aligns zone with Pod placement.

question: Which statement about in-tree storage drivers is TRUE in modern Kubernetes?

options.label: a
options.text: Most in-tree drivers have been migrated to CSI via CSI migration

explanation: CSI migration progressively redirects in-tree volume APIs (EBS, GCE PD, Azure Disk, etc.) to their CSI drivers so core can drop the in-tree code paths.

question: Which of the following is NOT true about a PersistentVolumeClaim?

options.label: b
options.text: It is a user request for storage of a particular size and access mode

options.label: c
options.text: It can trigger dynamic provisioning via a StorageClass

options.label: d
options.text: It binds to exactly one PV

explanation: PVCs are namespaced. PVs are cluster-scoped. A PVC binds 1:1 to a PV.
